summary: I was born and raised in Denver, Colorado in 1926. I went to Lincoln Elementary, Byers Junior High, and South High School here. Denver was where I spent my childhood, walking to school and enjoying holidays like the Fourth of July at the Denver Country Club. I also remember going to theaters on South Broadway and living near Kalamath Street where Hurt and Company was located.

I was born in Colorado, specifically Denver, in 1926. One memorable trip I took was with my grandmother up to Grand Junction, Colorado, to help a friend pick peaches. Growing up in Colorado shaped much of my early life experiences, from school to family outings.

My dad worked for ACME, specifically at the docks. It was called ACME Fast Freight, and that was part of our family life growing up in Denver. His work there was a steady part of our household background.

I attended Lincoln Elementary School from first through sixth grade. I loved school, and I remember my art teacher, Nicetrom, who was great, though I didn’t like my English teacher very much. Walking to Lincoln Elementary was about six blocks from home, and it was an important part of my early education in Denver.

After elementary school, I went to Byers Junior High School, which was within walking distance. I even skipped half a grade there, though I don’t remember much about it. Byers was the next step in my education before moving on to South High School.

I attended South High School in Denver because all my family went there. It was located near University Avenue, and I remember the rationing during World War II affecting our school life. I enjoyed math there and took some science, though I wasn’t fond of the science teacher.

During the war, I took welding classes at Emily Griffith Technical College while still in school. I was only sixteen then, and the skills I learned there helped me get hired later. Emily Griffith was a place where many of us bettered ourselves during World War II.

World War II was a significant backdrop to my teenage years. There was rationing of meat and other goods at school, and many of us took classes like welding to contribute to the war effort. The war shaped much of what I did during high school and afterward.

South Broadway was where we went to the movies, especially to the Weber and Mayan Theatres. We lived near there and even delivered flyers along the block from Alameda to First Avenue to get free tickets. It was a lively part of my childhood entertainment in Denver.

The Weber Theatre on South Broadway was one of the places I loved going to see movies as a kid. We earned free tickets by delivering flyers nearby, which made it a special treat. It was part of the fun times growing up in Denver during the 1930s and 40s.

The Mayan Theatre, also on South Broadway near First Avenue, was another favorite movie spot for me. I remember winning a Shirley Temple doll at one of the theaters, which I dearly loved. Going to the Mayan was a big part of my childhood entertainment.

Alameda Avenue was part of the route where we delivered flyers to get free movie tickets at the theaters on South Broadway. It was a familiar street in my neighborhood and part of the everyday life of a kid growing up in Denver.

I once won a Shirley Temple doll at one of the theaters on South Broadway, and I loved that doll dearly. I don’t remember what happened to it, but it was a treasured prize from my childhood. Shirley Temple was a big deal for kids like me back then.

Fourth of July was always a big celebration for us. My dad would buy a box of firecrackers, and we’d set them off, and we also went to the Denver Country Club to watch their fabulous fireworks. It was a joyful time with family and community in Denver.

Hurt and Company was just a half block from where I lived on Kalamath Street. After I graduated, I started working there, though I didn’t weld. It was close to home and part of my early work experience in Denver.

One memorable trip with my grandmother was to Grand Junction, Colorado, to help a friend pick peaches. It was a long car trip, and I remember the peach fuzz being terrible, but it was a special family outing. Grand Junction was a place I visited as a child outside of Denver.
